title: Swedish Meatballs

overview.description: Tender beef and pork meatballs gently seasoned with allspice and nutmeg, served in a rich creamy gravy. A classic Swedish comfort food traditionally accompanied by mashed potatoes and lingonberry jam.

method: Mix the beef mince, pork mince, grated onion, garlic, breadcrumbs, egg, milk, allspice, nutmeg, salt and pepper until just combined.
Heat 1 tablespoon of the butter in a large frying pan over a medium heat.
Brown the meatballs on all sides, working in batches if necessary. They do not need to be fully cooked at this stage.
Remove the meatballs and set aside.
Add the remaining butter to the pan, stir in the flour and cook for 1 minute.
Gradually whisk in the beef stock until smooth.
Stir in the double cream and Dijon mustard and bring to a gentle simmer.
Return the meatballs to the pan and simmer gently for 10–15 minutes until cooked through and the sauce has thickened.
Taste and adjust the seasoning before serving.

learning.did-you-know: Swedish meatballs are traditionally flavoured with warming spices like allspice, giving them a distinctive taste that sets them apart from Italian or British meatballs.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to make tender meatballs, build a simple pan gravy and balance rich flavours with traditional accompaniments.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A comforting family meal that's simple enough for a weekday but different enough to introduce Scandinavian flavours and techniques.
chapman-tips: Don't overwork the meat mixture or the meatballs can become dense.
Brown the meatballs well before making the gravy to build flavour.
Lingonberry jam is traditional, but cranberry sauce makes a reasonable substitute if you can't find it.
notes.batch-cooking: Freeze the cooked meatballs in their gravy for up to three months.
notes.leftovers: Reheat gently over a low heat, adding a splash of stock if the gravy becomes too thick.
